LAW AND MOTION 302, PLAINTIFF TONY FU'S MOTION FOR PRELIMINARY INJUNCTION AGAINST DEFT DEMAS YAN PURSUANT TO CCP 525-527 IS DENIED. NEITHER MR. FU'S NOTICE OF MOTION NOR HIS MEMORANDUM CLEARLY STATES THE PRECISE CONDUCT WHICH HE SEEKS TO HAVE ENJOINED. IT APPEARS, HOWEVER, THAT MR. FU DESIRES THAT THE COURT PROHIBIT MR. YAN FROM MAKING FALSE STATEMENTS ABOUT HIM WHICH CAUSE HIM HARM. AN INJUNCTION AGAINST SPEECH RAISES SERIOUS FIRST AMENDMENT CONCERNS OF PRIOR RESTRAINT. EVEN ASSUMING THAT THIS COURT COULD CONSTITUTIONALLY ENJOIN SPEECH BY MR. YAN, THERE ARE FURTHER SERIOUS CONCERNS ABOUT RESTRAINING MR. YAN'S ABILITY TO SPEAK TO THE POLICE OR THIRD PARTIES ABOUT ALLEGED CRIMINAL CONDUCT AS WELL AS FURTHER SERIOUS CONCERNS ABOUT RESTRAINING MR. YAN FROM MAKING STATEMENTS IN THE CONTEXT OF LITIGATION. BASED ON ALL THESE CONCERNS, THE COURT DEEMS IT HIGHLY IMPRUDENT TO ISSUE ANY INJUNCTION OF THE TYPE APPARENTLY DESIRED BY MR. FU, ESPECIALLY GIVEN THE RELATIVELY WEAK SHOWING OF THE FALSITY OF THE STATEMENTS MADE BY MR. YAN WHICH ARE THE PREDICATE FOR THE MOTION AND THE HARM CAUSED BY THOSE STATEMENTS. THE PARTIES ARE ADMONISHED THAT THEY SHOULD REFRAIN FROM MAKING ANY DEFAMATORY STATEMENTS ABOUT EACH OTHER. PREVAILING PARTY TO PREPARE ORDER IN COMPLIANCE WITH CRC 3.1312. Defendant Demas Yan?S Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order That Matters In Request For Admissions Be Deemed Admitted
Off Calendar Dec-02-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANT DEMAS YAN'S Motion For Order That Matters In Request For Admissions Be Deemed Admitted.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling. Defendant Demas Yan's motion for an order that matters in requests for admission be deemed admitted or alternatively compelling further responses to requests for admission and form interrogatories is denied. The requests for admission and form interrogatories were served on September 3, 2020. However, the discovery cut-off date in this case was 30 days "before the date initially set for the trial of the action," (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 2024.020(a)), which was November 28, 2016. The court's July 8, 2020 order resetting the trial date to January 25, 2021 did not reopen discovery. (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 2024.020(b) ["a continuance or postponement of the trial date does not operate to reopen discovery proceedings."]. Further, Defendant did not move to reopen discovery under Code of Civil Procedure section 2024.050 after the new trial date was set. Accordingly, discovery remains closed, and Plaintiff's objections on that ground are well-taken.

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported (302/EPS)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Oct-20-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

No appearance on this matter. The Court adopted its tentative ruling.
PLAINTIFF TONY FU'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication is DENIED in their entirety. Mr. Fu fails to maintain his summary judgment/adjudication burden of production. "A plaintiff or cross-complainant has met his or her burden of showing that there is no defense to a cause of action if that party has proved each element of the cause of action entitling the party to judgment on that cause of action." (CCP 437c(p)(1).) An element of plaintiff's causes of action for malicious prosecution and fraudulent transfer is damage. (Jackson v. Yarbray (2009) 179 Cal.App.4th 75, 90-97; CACI 1501(5) (harm to plaintiff element of malicious prosecution cause of action); CACI 4200(4) (harm to plaintiff element of fraudulent transfer cause of action).) Mr. Fu's separate statement of undisputed facts states that he "has rights to payment from Demas Yan" because of Mr. Yan's alleged misconduct. Mr. Fu's only supporting evidence-his declaration-fails to demonstrate any monetary damages and the declaration merely attaches prior court orders and judgments. The court cannot grant the motion without evidence of damages. Prevailing party to prepare a form of order and to comply with CCP 437c(g) and CRC 3.1312. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976 =302/hk

Notice Of Motion And Motion For An Order Declaring Tony Fu A Vexatious Litigant, For Security, And For Prefiling Order
Off Calendar Sep-19-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Tony Fu (415-867-5978) appears as plaintiff/opposing party

No other appearance is made in this matter.

The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

DEFENDANT DEMAS YAN'S Motion For An Order Declaring Tony Fu A Vexatious Litigant, For Security, And For Prefiling Order is DENIED. First, Yan fails to meet his burden under CCP 391(b)(1) by showing that five litigations were determined adversely to Fu within the past seven years. As to CGC-10-501321, the Court's register of actions shows the case was removed to federal court and Yan provides no further information. In CGC-13-531993, both sides (Fu and Mr. Shen) dismissed their claims; the reasons are unclear. CGC-15-545811 was consolidated with CGC-14-539494. While Fu dismissed his claim in that case, Ms. Lei (who was aligned with Fu) recovered more than $140,000. CGC-15-549575 is ongoing. Second, Yan also fails to meet his burden under CCP 391(b)(2). While Fu has filed repetitive actions, Yan does not show that the actions are frivolous; a remedy is to seek consolidation. Third, Yan also fails to meet his burden under CCP 391(b)(3); he does not show that Fu's prior motions/ filings were frivolous. Written order is signed and filed in open court.

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Preliminary Injunction Against Deft Demas Yan Pursuant To Ccp 525-527
Off Calendar Jul-18-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances.

The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

PLAINTIFF TONY FU's Motion For Preliminary Injunction Against Deft Demas Yan Pursuant To Ccp 525-527 is DENIED. Neither Mr. Fu's notice of motion nor his memorandum clearly states the precise conduct which he seeks to have enjoined. It appears, however, that Mr. Fu desires that the court prohibit Mr. Yan from making false statements about him which cause him harm. An injunction against speech raises serious First Amendment concerns of prior restraint. Even assuming that this court could constitutionally enjoin speech by Mr. Yan, there are further serious concerns about restraining Mr. Yan's ability to speak to the police or third parties about alleged criminal conduct as well as further serious concerns about restraining Mr. Yan from making statements in the context of litigation. Based on all these concerns, the court deems it highly imprudent to issue any injunction of the type apparently desired by Mr. Fu, especially given the relatively weak showing of the falsity of the statements made by Mr. Yan which are the predicate for the motion and the harm caused by those statements. The parties are admonished that they should refrain from making any defamatory statements about each other. Prevailing party to prepare order in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Jose Rios Merida; Reporter: Reyes F. Hunter, CSR #6576, Bus. Tel: 530-339-5023. (D302)

Defendants Notice Of Motion And Special Motion To Strike Pursuant To Code Of Civil Procedure §425.16 (Anti-Slapp)
Off Calendar Apr-26-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances: Plaintiff Tony Fu telephone number 415-867-5973 in Propria Persona.
Emily Charley of Hanson Bridgett telephone number 415-777-3200 for Defendants Allen Matkins Leck Gamble Mallory & Natsis LLP and Michael Betz.

The matter was argued. Upon conclusion of argument, the Court confirmed its tenative ruling. DEFENDANTS ALLEN MATKINS LECK GAMBLE MALLORY & NATSIS LLP, and MICHAEL BETZ'S Special Motion To Strike particular paragraphs in the complaint is denied without prejudice to refiling an anti-SLAPP motion to strike directed to the entirety of the third cause of action. While there is a split of authority and the issue is presently pending before the California Supreme Court, this court believes that the better-reasoned view is that an anti-SLAPP motion to strike may not seek to strike less than the entirety of a claim. (Compare Mann v. Quality Old Time Service, Inc. (2004) 120 Cal. App. 4th 90 with Cho v. Chang (2013) 219 Cal. App. 4th 521). For guidance of the parties but without making a ruling on a motion not yet made, the court notes that plaintiff Tony Fu may wish to dismiss the third cause of action against the Allen Matkins firm and Mr. Betz before they file another anti-SLAPP motion to strike since it appears that the third cause of action is based on non-incidental allegations of fact that may constitute protected activity per CCP 425.16 and the third cause of action may be barred by the litigation privilege and the statute of limitations for legal malpractice actions. The court further notes that the prior rulings denying an anti-SLAPP motion to strike the second cause of action based on asserted fraudulent transfers outside of court are not applicable to an anti-SLAPP motion to strike the third cause of action based on asserted false filings in a court action and legal malpractice. For purposes of anti-SLAPP analysis, the two claims are very different, even though they both may be labeled fraud claims by Mr. Fu. Order signed in open Court.

Judge: Harold Kahn. Clerk: Rosie Noguera. Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976. (302/HK)

MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Apr-08-2016 Denied
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT DEMAS YAN'S ANTI-SLAPP (CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE 425.16) MOTION TO STRIKE AND CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE 435 MOTION TO STRIKE

Appearances: Tony Fu (415.867.5973) plaintiff and moving party appearing in propria persona; No other appearances.

This matter was not argued. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Denied in their entirety. The malicious prosecution cause of action implicates section 425.16, but the fraud claim does not. Plaintiff demonstrates a probability of prevailing on the malicious prosecution cause of action. Defendant filed a cross-complaint in case no CGC-10-501321. That cross-complaint attempted to re-litigate the Chenery Project issues. The bankruptcy court rulings show that the cross-complaint lacked merit because the claims had already been resolved and only the trustee-not defendant-could assert them. On March 18, 2014, the Ninth Circuit affirmed the bankruptcy rulings and found defendant's appeal to be frivolous. Plaintiff timely filed the instant action on December 23, 2015. The cross-complaint favorably terminated in plaintiff's favor, the bankruptcy court rulings show that there was no probable cause to bring the cross-complaint, and defendant's vexatious litigation shows that he was maintaining the cross-complaint for an improper purpose, which demonstrates malice. (Sierra Club Foundation v. Graham (1999) 72 Cal.App.4th 1135, 1157.) The section 435 motion to strike is denied because none of the allegations sought to be stricken are clearly irrelevant or prejudicial. While plaintiff makes reference to lawsuits that in and of themselves cannot be the basis for a malicious prosecution claim, those lawsuits may be relevant to show malice or lack of probable cause. Order signed. Judge: Harold E. Kahn, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Not Reported
